🟡 Cycle syncing
Tailoring diet, exercise, and treatment to menstrual cycle phases. Evidence growing for phase-specific nutrition: follicular = lighter cardio + raw foods; luteal = strength training + warming cooked foods. Seed cycling: flaxseed + pumpkin (follicular phase) → sesame + sunflower (luteal phase) supports oestrogen then progesterone balance via lignans and zinc.

cards.title: Oestrogen Dominance — Assessment & Management
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition
Oestrogen dominance = elevated oestrogen relative to progesterone. Can occur with normal oestrogen if progesterone is low. Increasingly common due to xenoestrogens, obesity, stress, and gut dysbiosis.
1
Symptoms — Heavy/painful periods, breast tenderness, fibroids, endometriosis, PMS, weight gain (hips/thighs), mood swings, thyroid disruption, fatigue
2
Root causes — ↑ body fat (aromatase), ↓ liver clearance, ↑ beta-glucuronidase (gut), xenoestrogens (plastics, pesticides, parabens), chronic stress (↓ progesterone), alcohol
3
Lab testing — Day 21 serum progesterone, oestradiol (E2), oestrone (E1), DUTCH test (oestrogen metabolite ratio: 2-OH:16-OH target >2:1)
4
Treatment — DIM (diindolylmethane) 200mg, calcium D-glucarate 500mg, flaxseed 2 tbsp/day, I3C from broccoli, liver support (milk thistle), reduce alcohol + xenoestrogens
🟡 2-OH:16-OH oestrogen ratio
Oestrogen is metabolised via two main pathways: 2-hydroxylation (protective) and 16α-hydroxylation (proliferative). Target ratio >2:1 (2-OH:16-OH). Cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts) shift metabolism toward protective 2-OH pathway via DIM and I3C. DUTCH test is the only way to measure this ratio clinically. Low ratio = elevated breast and endometrial cancer risk.

cards.title: PCOS — Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Rotterdam criteria (2 of 3)
Oligo-ovulation, Hyperandrogenism, PCO on USS

8–13% of reproductive-age women

Present in 70% of PCOS

PCOS TypeFeaturesPrimary Naturopathic Focus
Insulin-resistant (Type 1)Overweight, acanthosis nigricans, ↑ fasting insulinLow-GI diet, inositol ★, berberine, exercise, chromium
Post-pill (Type 2)Normal weight, post-OCP, elevated LHVitex agnus-castus 4–6 months, liver support, zinc

🟡 Inositol — best evidence in PCOS
Myo-inositol 4g/day (or myo:D-chiro ratio 40:1) is the best-evidenced naturopathic intervention for insulin-resistant PCOS. RCTs show: ↓ fasting insulin, ↓ testosterone, ↑ ovulation rate, ↑ fertility (comparable to metformin with fewer side effects). Mechanism: insulin second messenger, restores FSH signalling. Takes 3–6 months. Combine with alpha-lipoic acid 600mg for enhanced effect.

🟡 Black cohosh (Cimicifuga racemosa)
Best-evidenced herbal for hot flushes. Not oestrogenic (does not bind oestrogen receptors) — serotonergic and dopaminergic mechanism. Safe in oestrogen-sensitive cancers (unlike phytoestrogens). Dose: 40–80mg standardised extract (2.5% triterpene glycosides) daily. Takes 4–8 weeks. Rare hepatotoxicity reported (monitor LFTs if using >6 months). Isopropanolic extract (Remifemin) is best-studied brand.

cards.title: Breast Health — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Breast Cancer — Screening is Life-Saving ★
Mammography screening: 40–74 years, every 2 years (India: every year from 40 with high risk). CBE (clinical breast examination) annually. Self-breast examination monthly (7–10 days after period). Any new breast lump, nipple discharge, skin change, or axillary lymphadenopathy → urgent referral.
ConditionFeaturesAssessmentNaturopathic Approach
Fibrocystic breast changesBilateral cyclical breast pain, lumpiness — worse premenstrually. Very common (60% of women)Clinical + USS if discrete lump. Biopsy if suspiciousEvening primrose oil 3g/day (GLA — Grade B), Vit E 400IU, reduce caffeine, low-fat diet, Vit B6 100mg
Mastalgia (breast pain)Cyclical (hormonal) or non-cyclical (musculoskeletal, ductal)Pain diary — cyclical vs non-cyclical, mammogram if >35Evening primrose oil, Vit B6, well-fitting supportive bra, Vit E, reduce dietary fat (saturated), danazol (prescriptive for severe)
Galactorrhoea (non-puerperal)Milky nipple discharge when not pregnant/breastfeedingProlactin level, MRI pituitary (r/o prolactinoma), TSH (hypothyroidism)Identify cause — if medication-induced (antipsychotics, metoclopramide) review with prescriber. Vitex (Chaste Tree) if hormonal — reduces prolactin
Breast cancer preventionLifestyle risk reductionBRCA1/2 testing if family history. BIRADS scoring on imagingMediterranean diet (↓ risk 25%), exercise 150 min/week (↓ risk 20%), maintain healthy weight (post-menopausal obesity ↑ oestrogen), limit alcohol (each drink/day ↑ risk 7%), breastfeed if possible
🟡 Phytoestrogens & Breast Cancer — Current Evidence
Phytoestrogens (soy isoflavones) were historically feared in ER+ breast cancer — concern that they would stimulate tumour growth. Current evidence is reassuring: dietary soy (tofu, edamame, miso) is safe and may be PROTECTIVE — Asian women who eat soy throughout life have lower breast cancer rates. However, high-dose soy supplements in ER+ breast cancer patients should be discussed with the oncologist before use. The evidence for harm is from animal studies with isolated isoflavones at very high doses — not from dietary soy. Key message: whole food soy = safe. High-dose isolated isoflavone supplements in ER+ cancer = discuss with oncologist first.

cards.title: Cervical Health & HPV — Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Cervical Cancer — 99% Caused by HPV — Preventable ★
HPV (Human Papillomavirus) — especially strains 16 and 18 — causes 99% of cervical cancers. Most HPV infections clear spontaneously within 2 years. Persistent infection → cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) → cancer over 10–20 years.
ScreeningScheduleNotes
Pap smear (cytology)Every 3 years from age 21 (or 3 years after first sexual activity)India: every 3 years in public health. Private: annually recommended
HPV co-testEvery 5 years from age 30 (Pap + HPV DNA)Preferred by current guidelines — higher sensitivity than Pap alone
HPV vaccination9–26 years (2 doses if <15, 3 doses if >15). Some guidelines extend to 45Gardasil 9 — protects against 9 HPV strains including 16, 18, 6, 11. Safe and highly effective — 98% protection
Naturopathic SupportDoseEvidenceMechanism
Folate (methylfolate)800mcg–5mg/day★★★ GoodDNA methylation and repair — folate deficiency impairs HPV clearance
Vitamin C1–2g/day★★★ GoodAntiviral, immune support, cervical mucosal integrity
Vitamin A (beta-carotene)10,000 IU beta-carotene/day (NOT retinol — teratogenic)★★★ GoodEpithelial differentiation — deficiency associated with CIN progression
Green tea (EGCG)3–5 cups/day or EGCG 400mg/day. Topical EGCG ointment for CIN 2 (prescriptive)★★★ GoodEGCG inhibits HPV E6/E7 oncoproteins — promotes apoptosis of abnormal cells
Indole-3-carbinol (DIM)200–300mg/day★★★ GoodPromotes favourable oestrogen metabolism (2-OHE1 over 16-OHE1), reduces CIN regression time
Immune supportZinc 15mg + Selenium 200mcg + Vit D 4000IU★★★ GoodImmune clearance of HPV — low immunity = persistent infection
🟡 CIN Management — When to Refer
CIN 1 = watchful waiting + naturopathic support (60% spontaneous regression at 2 years). CIN 2 = naturopathic support + 6-monthly colposcopy. Regression possible especially in women under 30. CIN 3 = REFER immediately for LLETZ/cone biopsy — risk of progression to cancer is too high to delay. Key lifestyle factors that promote HPV clearance: smoking cessation (smoking impairs local cervical immunity — most important modifiable factor), safe sex practices, immune support, stress management. Naturopathy can genuinely help CIN 1–2 regression — this is an area of real clinical opportunity.

cards.title: Pelvic Floor Health — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Pelvic Floor — The Hidden Foundation
The pelvic floor is a hammock of muscles supporting bladder, uterus, and rectum. Dysfunction affects 1 in 3 women who have given birth and is massively underdiagnosed. It causes: UI, pelvic organ prolapse, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and fecal incontinence — all treatable.
ConditionFeaturesPelvic Floor StatusApproach
Stress urinary incontinenceLeakage with exertion/cough/sneezeUnderactive/weakKegel exercises, pelvic floor PT, pessary, weight loss
Urge incontinence (OAB)Sudden urge, cannot delay voidingOveractive detrusorBladder training, pelvic floor PT, neuromodulation
Pelvic organ prolapsePressure/heaviness in pelvis, "something coming down", worse at end of dayWeak + laxPelvic floor PT, pessary (ring pessary), surgical repair if severe (Grades 3–4)
VaginismusInvoluntary vaginal muscle spasm — penetration impossible or painful, primary or secondaryHypertonic (too tight)Pelvic floor PT + psychosexual therapy + gradual dilator therapy. Never Kegels — worsens hypertonic floor
Vulvodynia/dyspareuniaChronic vulval pain, burning, stinging — no identifiable causeOften hypertonic + neural sensitisationPelvic floor PT, low-oxalate diet, topical lidocaine, amitriptyline (prescriptive), CBT for pain
K
Kegel Exercise Protocol (for Underactive Floor) Identify: squeeze as if stopping urinary stream AND holding wind simultaneously. 3-second hold × 10 reps (fast twitch) + 10-second hold × 10 reps (slow twitch) = 1 set. 3 sets/day. Progress weekly. IMPORTANT: only for underactive/weak floors — contraindicated in hypertonic pelvic floor (vaginismus, vulvodynia, chronic pelvic pain).
N
Naturopathic Support for Pelvic Health Magnesium glycinate 300mg — reduces detrusor overactivity and pelvic muscle tension. Vit D 4000 IU — pelvic floor muscle function. Omega-3 3g — reduces pelvic neuroinflammation. Pumpkin seed extract 500mg BD — Grade B for OAB. Low-oxalate diet for vulvodynia (oxalate crystals irritate vulval tissue). Topical coconut oil — safe lubricant, reduces dryness and friction.
🟡 Refer to Pelvic Floor Physiotherapist
Pelvic floor physiotherapy is the most evidence-based treatment for most pelvic floor disorders — superior to general physiotherapy and equally effective as surgery for Grade 1–2 prolapse. Unfortunately massively under-utilised. Criteria for referral: any leakage affecting quality of life, prolapse symptoms, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, vaginismus, post-partum (should be routine at 6-week check), post-prostatectomy (men). The assessment involves internal examination — ensure patient is prepared and consents fully. A trained pelvic floor physiotherapist can distinguish hypertonic from hypotonic floor — critical for correct treatment.

cards.title: Breast Health & Female Osteoporosis
cards._legacyHtml:
BREAST HEALTH
Breast Cancer Screening ★
Mammography every 2 years from age 40. CBE annually. Self-breast examination monthly (days 7–10 after period). Any new lump, nipple discharge, skin change → urgent referral.
ConditionFeaturesNaturopathic Approach
Fibrocystic breast changesBilateral cyclical pain, lumpiness — worse premenstrually. Very common (60% of women)Evening primrose oil 3g/day (GLA), Vit E 400IU, reduce caffeine, Vit B6 100mg, low-fat diet
Mastalgia (cyclical)Premenstrual breast pain — hormonalEvening primrose oil, Vitex 400mg AM, well-fitting supportive bra, reduce dietary fat
Breast cancer preventionLifestyle risk reductionMediterranean diet (↓ risk 25%), exercise 150 min/week (↓ risk 20%), maintain healthy BMI, alcohol <1 drink/day (each drink ↑ risk 7%), breastfeed if possible
🟡 Phytoestrogens & ER+ Breast Cancer — Current Evidence
Dietary soy (tofu, edamame, miso) is SAFE and likely PROTECTIVE in ER+ breast cancer — Asian women with lifelong soy consumption have lower breast cancer rates. High-dose isolated isoflavone supplements in active ER+ cancer treatment: discuss with oncologist before prescribing. Phytoestrogens bind ER with lower affinity than oestradiol and act as partial agonists/antagonists — dietary amounts are not a concern.
OSTEOPOROSIS IN WOMEN
InterventionDoseEvidence
Calcium ★1200mg/day total (food + supplement) in split doses★★★★ — food sources preferred (dairy, sardines, tofu, leafy greens)
Vitamin D3 ★2000–4000 IU/day — target 25(OH)D >75 nmol/L★★★★ — must combine with calcium
Vitamin K2 (MK-7)100–200mcg/day★★★ — directs calcium to bone, away from arteries
Resistance training ★2–3x/week all major muscle groups★★★★ — mechanical loading activates osteoblasts
Collagen peptides10g/day★★★ — improves BMD + muscle mass in postmenopausal women (RCT)
Isoflavones (post-menopausal)80–90mg/day soy isoflavones★★★ — oestrogenic effect reduces bone resorption

Probiotic L.rhamnosus + L.reuteri vaginal or oral — reduces recurrence. Boric acid 600mg vaginal capsules 14 days for recurrent BV.

Naturopathic: boric acid 600mg vaginal suppository OD x14 days (evidence for recurrent — especially non-albicans Candida). Probiotics. Reduce dietary sugar and refined carbs. Tea tree oil sitz bath (2 drops in warm water — not undiluted).

🟡 Recurrent Candida — Investigation Protocol
Recurrent candida (4+ episodes/year) requires investigation: blood glucose and HbA1c (undiagnosed T2DM is most common cause), HIV testing, iron deficiency (impairs mucosal immunity), thyroid function, check all medications (antibiotics, steroids, immunosuppressants, OCP). Culture the organism — 15% of recurrent cases are non-albicans species (C.glabrata, C.krusei) which are inherently resistant to fluconazole but sensitive to boric acid 600mg vaginal capsules. Probiotic L.rhamnosus GR-1 + L.reuteri RC-14 — specific strains with evidence for vaginal microbiome restoration.

cards.title: Contraception — Naturopathic Perspective & Effects
cards._legacyHtml:
Naturopathic Role in Contraception
BNYS practitioners do not prescribe contraception but must understand its effects, nutritional implications, and how to support patients on hormonal contraception. Many patients also seek naturopathic alternatives or wish to transition off hormonal contraception.
MethodNutritional DepletionsNaturopathic SupportSide Effects to Address
Combined oral contraceptive pill (OCP)B6, B12, folate, zinc, magnesium, selenium, CoQ10, Vit CB-complex daily, zinc 15mg, magnesium 300mg, Vit C 500mg, CoQ10 100mg throughout OCP useMood changes (B6, Mg), libido (zinc, DHEA), headaches (magnesium), nausea (ginger, B6)
Progestogen-only pill (POP/Mini-pill)Similar to OCP but less severeB-complex, zinc, magnesiumIrregular bleeding, mood, acne — DIM/I3C for oestrogen metabolism
Hormonal IUD (Mirena)Minimal — localised progestogenMonitor iron (can cause or worsen anaemia, or improve heavy periods)Irregular spotting (iron monitoring), mood effects in sensitive women
Copper IUDMay increase menstrual blood loss — ironMonitor ferritin annually. Iron bisglycinate if ferritin low. Zinc if deficient.Heavier periods — see menorrhagia naturopathic protocol
Natural family planning (NFP)NoneCycle tracking (Creighton, Billings, symptothermal method), basal body temperature, LH testing. Effectiveness 76-98% depending on method and correct use.N/A — requires consistent tracking and abstinence during fertile window
🟡 Post-Pill Syndrome
After stopping OCP, some women experience: amenorrhoea (up to 6 months), acne rebound, PMS intensification, hair shedding (telogen effluvium from hormonal shift), mood changes, and nutritional deficiency symptoms. Support protocol: B-complex, zinc, magnesium, Vitex (after 3 months off pill — regulates cycle), seed cycling (flaxseed and pumpkin follicular phase; sesame and sunflower luteal phase — modest hormonal modulation). Most symptoms resolve within 3-6 months. Prolactin level if amenorrhoea persists beyond 6 months.

cards.title: Sexual Health & Female Libido — Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Female Sexual Dysfunction — Underdiagnosed
Female sexual interest/arousal disorder (FSIAD) affects 40% of women at some point. Low libido, dyspareunia (painful sex), anorgasmia. Always explore: hormonal, psychological, relational, medication, and anatomical factors.
CauseAssessmentNaturopathic Intervention
Hormonal (low testosterone)Total and free testosterone, SHBG, DHEA-S. Free testosterone often low even with normal total (high SHBG binds testosterone).DHEA 25-50mg/day (SHBG ↓, free testosterone ↑). Vitex (lowers prolactin if elevated). Tribulus terrestris 250mg TDS (modest libido evidence). Zinc 15mg (testosterone cofactor).
Menopausal (atrophy and dryness)GSM symptoms — dryness, dyspareunia, vulval atrophySea buckthorn oil 3g/day oral (RCT — improves vaginal mucosa). Topical Vit E. Coconut oil or hyaluronic acid lubricant. Topical oestrogen (refer GP — gold standard).
OCP-inducedSHBG elevated on OCP (binds testosterone) — libido often returns after stoppingZinc, B6, Mg during OCP. Consider non-hormonal contraception if libido significantly impaired.
Antidepressant-inducedSSRIs cause sexual dysfunction in 50-70% — anorgasmia, low desireMaca root 3g/day (RCT — specifically reverses SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction). Discuss with prescriber about switching to bupropion or mirtazapine (lower sexual side effect profile).
Psychological/relationalRelationship quality, trauma history, body image, anxiety, depressionCBT for sexual dysfunction, EMDR for sexual trauma, couples therapy, mindfulness-based sex therapy (Brotto protocol). Refer psychosexual therapist.
Dyspareunia (painful sex)Pelvic examination (vaginismus, atrophy, endometriosis, infections)Pelvic floor physiotherapy, lubricants, treat underlying cause. Never ignore dyspareunia — always assess.

cards.title: Osteoporosis in Women — Risk, DEXA & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Osteoporosis — Silent Disease Until Fracture
1 in 3 women over 50 will have an osteoporotic fracture. Hip fracture 1-year mortality is 20-30%. Prevention starts decades before — peak bone mass is achieved at age 25-30, then gradually declines.
Risk FactorModifiableNon-Modifiable
Major risksLow calcium and Vit D intake, smoking, alcohol, low body weight, inactivity, long-term steroids or PPIsAge, female sex, menopause, prior fragility fracture, family history, white or Asian ethnicity
Secondary causesCoeliac disease, IBD, hyperparathyroidism, thyrotoxicosis, rheumatoid arthritis, T1DM, early menopause (under 45), prolonged amenorrhoea, aromatase inhibitors
InterventionDoseEvidence
Calcium1200mg/day total (food preferred + supplement). Max 500mg per dose.Strong — with Vit D3
Vitamin D32000-4000 IU/day. Target 25(OH)D above 75 nmol/L.Strong — essential with calcium
Vitamin K2 (MK-7)100-200mcg/dayGood — directs calcium to bone, prevents vascular calcification
Magnesium300-400mg/dayGood — cofactor for Vit D activation and bone matrix proteins
Resistance training2-3x/week all major muscle groupsStrong — most potent stimulus for bone formation (mechanical loading activates osteoblasts)
Collagen peptides10g/dayGood — improves bone mineral density and muscle mass in postmenopausal women (RCT)
Soy isoflavones80-90mg/day (postmenopausal)Good — oestrogenic effect on bone, reduces resorption
🟡 FRAX Score
Use FRAX calculator (shef.ac.uk/FRAX) for every patient with osteopenia or risk factors. FRAX calculates 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture and hip fracture — guides treatment decisions. Consider bisphosphonates if 10-year hip fracture risk over 3% or major fracture risk over 20%, alongside naturopathic protocol. Always investigate and treat secondary causes first — untreated coeliac disease or hyperparathyroidism makes osteoporosis treatment ineffective.

cards.title: Thyroid — Functional Assessment
cards._legacyHtml:
Thyroid in Women — The Overlooked Driver
Women are 8× more likely than men to develop thyroid disease. Subclinical hypothyroidism affects 10% of women over 50. Thyroid dysfunction profoundly affects: menstrual regularity, fertility, mood, weight, cognition, skin, hair, and cardiovascular risk — making thyroid the most important endocrine screen in women's health.
ParameterStandard RangeFunctional OptimalClinical Notes
TSH0.5–5.0 mIU/L1.0–2.5 mIU/LTSH >2.5 in symptomatic woman = treat naturopathically, consider levothyroxine. TSH >4.0 = strong treatment indication.
Free T410–22 pmol/L15–20 pmol/L (upper half)Low-normal fT4 + symptoms = insufficient T4 production
Free T33.5–6.5 pmol/L5.0–6.5 pmol/LLow fT3 with normal TSH = poor T4→T3 conversion (selenium, zinc, iron deficiency, high cortisol, inflammation)
Reverse T3 (rT3)<0.44 nmol/L<0.3 nmol/LrT3 elevated in: chronic stress, low-calorie dieting, inflammation, iron deficiency, selenium deficiency
Anti-TPO antibodies<35 IU/mL<10 IU/mLElevated = Hashimoto's. Selenium 200mcg/day ★★★ reduces by 50%.
1
Naturopathic Thyroid Optimisation for WomenSelenium 200mcg/day ★★★ (reduces TPO antibodies, supports T4→T3 conversion via deiodinase type 2). Zinc 15mg/day (deiodinase cofactor — zinc deficiency impairs T3 conversion). Iron: ferritin >70 essential for deiodinase function (low iron = low T3 despite adequate T4). Iodine: 150mcg/day from food/prenatal vitamins (not supplements — excess worsens Hashimoto's). Vit D 3000–5000 IU ★★★ (VDR deficiency drives autoimmune thyroid disease). Manage cortisol: chronic cortisol ↑ rT3 and ↓ active T3 — HPA support is thyroid support.

cards.title: Endometriosis — Integrative Management
cards._legacyHtml:
Endometriosis — 10 Years Average Diagnostic Delay
Endometriosis affects 10% of reproductive-age women (176 million globally). Average diagnostic delay is 7–10 years because: (1) period pain is normalised, (2) symptoms overlap with IBS/bladder, (3) laparoscopy required for diagnosis. BNYS role: pain management, anti-inflammatory support, hormone balancing, and quality of life improvement alongside medical/surgical management.
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
Omega-3 3–4g/day★★★ Reduces prostaglandin E2 and leukotriene B4 — primary inflammatory mediators in endometriosis. Reduces dysmenorrhoea comparable to NSAIDs in some RCTs.EPA+DHA 3–4g/day. Start 2 weeks before menstruation for dysmenorrhoea. Continue daily for systemic anti-inflammatory effect.
DIM (diindolylmethane)★★★ Shifts oestrogen metabolism from 16-OH (pro-proliferative) to 2-OH (protective). Reduces ectopic tissue growth in animal models. Human pilot data promising.DIM 200–400mg/day. From cruciferous vegetables (broccoli sprouts, cauliflower) — 500g/day would be required from food; supplement preferred for therapeutic dose.
Resveratrol★★ Inhibits aromatase (reduces local oestrogen production in ectopic tissue), anti-inflammatory, anti-angiogenicResveratrol 30mg/day BD (trans-resveratrol from Japanese knotweed or grape skin extract). Synergistic with curcumin.
Low-FODMAP + anti-inflammatory diet★★★ 72% of endometriosis patients have IBS-type symptoms (shared visceral hypersensitivity). Low-FODMAP reduces bloating and pain. Anti-inflammatory pattern reduces inflammatory load.Mediterranean anti-inflammatory diet base. Low-FODMAP trial 4–6 weeks if GI symptoms prominent. Eliminate: red meat, trans fats, gluten (controversial but reported to help). Add: turmeric, ginger, colourful vegetables.
Pelvic physiotherapy★★★ Addresses hypertonic pelvic floor (common in endo — protective guarding response). Internal manual therapy, trigger points, myofascial release. Often dramatically reduces dyspareunia and pelvic pain.Refer to specialist pelvic floor physiotherapist. 6–12 sessions. Combined with yoga (yin yoga for pelvic release ★★). Home stretching: pigeon, reclined butterfly, supine figure-4.

cards.title: PMS & PMDD — Evidence-Based Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
PMS
Physical + mood symptoms, luteal phase, impair daily function mildly
PMDD
Severe mood symptoms (ICD-11/DSM-5), luteal phase, marked functional impairment
Diagnosis
Prospective symptom diary × 2 cycles minimum. DRSP (Daily Record of Severity of Problems)
PMDD treatment
SSRIs (luteal phase dosing) are first-line conventional. Always refer PMDD to gynaecology.
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
Magnesium glycinate ★Grade A — multiple RCTs ↓ physical and mood symptoms360–400mg/day throughout cycle. Especially day 15–28 for mood.

cards.title: Perimenopause Hormone Testing
cards._legacyHtml:
Perimenopause Hormone Testing — Why Serum Is Often Unreliable
Perimenopause (typically 40–52 years) is characterised by erratic, fluctuating hormone levels — FSH and oestradiol vary dramatically from day to day and even within the same day. A single blood test can be completely normal one day and severely abnormal the next. Diagnosis is primarily clinical — blood tests confirm but cannot exclude perimenopause.
Hormone TestUtility in PerimenopauseInterpretationLimitations
FSH (Day 2–5 of cycle or random in amenorrhoea)Confirms menopause if: FSH >40 IU/L on 2 occasions ≥4 weeks apart + no menses for 12 monthsIn perimenopause: FSH fluctuates widely. High FSH one month may normalise next month. FSH >10 = diminished ovarian reserve.Cannot confirm PERI-menopause (pre-cessation) — only post-menopausal state. A normal FSH does NOT exclude perimenopause.
Oestradiol (E2)Highly variable in perimenopause — can be very high (follicular bursts), low, or normal on the SAME dayFollicular phase normal: 77–921 pmol/L. Post-menopause: <140 pmol/L (stable, low). Perimenopausal: unpredictable.Not reliable for perimenopause diagnosis. Used for monitoring HRT dosing and in PCOS/fertility assessment.
AMH (Anti-Müllerian Hormone)Best single marker of ovarian reserve — reflects remaining primordial follicle poolAge-specific ranges. AMH <0.4 ng/mL = very low ovarian reserve. AMH <1.0 ng/mL = diminished reserve. AMH >3.5 ng/mL = polycystic ovary pattern.Does NOT change throughout the menstrual cycle (unlike FSH/E2) — can be measured any time. Decreases smoothly with age — the best reserve predictor.
DUTCH Test (Dried Urine)Gold standard for full sex hormone profiling — oestrogen, progesterone, testosterone, cortisol, DHEAS, melatonin + metabolitesShows: oestrogen metabolism pathways (2-OH vs 4-OH vs 16-OH oestrogens). Cortisol-DHEA ratio. Complete hormonal picture unavailable from serum.Expensive (₹10,000–15,000 in India). Not universally available. Complex to interpret — requires trained practitioner. Most valuable in: unexplained symptoms with normal serum, assessment for cancer risk oestrogen metabolite profile.
🟡 The Symptom-Based Approach
The most experienced menopause specialists (Menopause Society) advise: in a woman over 45 with typical perimenopause symptoms (irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms, mood changes, sleep disruption), blood tests are not required to make the clinical diagnosis or start treatment. Symptoms + age + menstrual irregularity = perimenopause. Blood tests are indicated for: women under 40 (premature ovarian insufficiency — different management), to guide HRT dosing, to exclude other causes (thyroid, anaemia, pregnancy), and for fertility counselling. Never withhold treatment based on a normal hormone test in a symptomatic perimenopausal woman.

cards.title: HRT — Risks, Benefits & Naturopathic Context
cards._legacyHtml:
HRT — Re-Evaluation After the WHI Study Clarification
The 2002 WHI study caused massive HRT abandonment — but the study used oral conjugated equine oestrogen + medroxyprogesterone acetate in older (average age 63) post-menopausal women, not perimenopausal women starting HRT at menopause. Re-analysis showed dramatically different risk-benefit profile for women starting HRT within 10 years of menopause onset ("timing hypothesis"). The conversation has fundamentally changed.
FactorDetailClinical Implication
Timing HypothesisHRT started within 10 years of menopause or before age 60 = cardiovascular BENEFIT (not risk). Started >10 years after = possible cardiovascular risk.Perimenopausal women asking about HRT: timing strongly favours benefit for bone, heart, cognitive function, and vasomotor symptoms. Refer to experienced menopause clinician.
Route of administrationTransdermal oestrogen: no ↑ VTE risk (oral oestrogen = 2–3× ↑ VTE, transdermal = neutral for VTE risk)Transdermal (patch, gel) preferred over oral for VTE safety. Especially important in women with obesity, smoking, thrombophilia risk.
Progestogen typeMicronised progesterone (Utrogestan/Prometrium): neutral cardiovascular profile, no ↑ breast cancer vs synthetic progestogens (MPA). WHI used MPA — the problematic progestogen.Body-identical progesterone preferred over synthetic progestogens. Micronised progesterone also improves sleep and anxiety.
Breast cancer riskOestrogen-only HRT (hysterectomised): slightly DECREASES breast cancer risk. Combined HRT: small increase (equivalent to: drinking 1–2 glasses wine/night, BMI >30, having no children, or being sedentary)Contextualise breast cancer risk: other modifiable lifestyle factors (alcohol, obesity, inactivity) carry comparable or higher risk than combined HRT started at appropriate age.
1
Naturopathic Alternatives & Adjuncts to HRT Phytoestrogens (non-HRT alternatives): Genistein (soy) 54mg/day ★★★ (reduces hot flushes 50% vs 25% placebo — only isoflavone with consistent RCT evidence). Equol (soy metabolite — only produced by ~50% of women with right gut bacteria) ★★★. Red clover isoflavones 40–80mg ★★. Flaxseed 25g/day ★★ (phytoestrogenic lignans + omega-3 + fibre). Remifemin (black cohosh extract) 40mg BD ★★★ (KappaE-ligand mechanism — not oestrogenic — safe in ER+ cancer per current evidence). Ashwagandha ★★ (reduces hot flush severity via HPA modulation, improves sleep). Lifestyle: weight loss (reduces oestrogen from adipose conversion of androgens → reduces hot flushes). Exercise ★★★ (most consistent lifestyle intervention for vasomotor symptom reduction). Yoga ★★★.

cards.title: Interstitial Cystitis & Bladder Pain Syndrome
cards._legacyHtml:
IC/BPS — Frequently Misdiagnosed
Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) affects 3–8 million women in the US alone. Symptoms: urinary urgency, frequency (>8 times/day), nocturia, and pelvic pain — WITHOUT infection or other identifiable cause. Frequently misdiagnosed as UTI, OAB, vulvodynia, or endometriosis. Average time to correct diagnosis: 5–7 years. Naturopathic interventions show meaningful efficacy.

Reintroduce one at a time after baseline established.Food triggers irritate damaged glycosaminoglycan (GAG) layer of bladder wall
Quercetin (CystoProtek)★★★ RCT: Quercetin 500mg BD significantly reduces IC symptom score (ICSI + ICPI)Quercetin 500mg BD. cards.title: Oestrogen Dominance — Assessment & Management
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition
Oestrogen dominance = elevated oestrogen relative to progesterone. Can occur with normal oestrogen if progesterone is low. Increasingly common due to xenoestrogens, obesity, stress, and gut dysbiosis.
1
Symptoms — Heavy/painful periods, breast tenderness, fibroids, endometriosis, PMS, weight gain (hips/thighs), mood swings, thyroid disruption, fatigue
2
Root causes — ↑ body fat (aromatase), ↓ liver clearance, ↑ beta-glucuronidase (gut), xenoestrogens (plastics, pesticides, parabens), chronic stress (↓ progesterone), alcohol
3
Lab testing — Day 21 serum progesterone, oestradiol (E2), oestrone (E1), DUTCH test (oestrogen metabolite ratio: 2-OH:16-OH target >2:1)
4
Treatment — DIM (diindolylmethane) 200mg, calcium D-glucarate 500mg, flaxseed 2 tbsp/day, I3C from broccoli, liver support (milk thistle), reduce alcohol + xenoestrogens
🟡 2-OH:16-OH oestrogen ratio
Oestrogen is metabolised via two main pathways: 2-hydroxylation (protective) and 16α-hydroxylation (proliferative). Target ratio >2:1 (2-OH:16-OH). Cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts) shift metabolism toward protective 2-OH pathway via DIM and I3C. DUTCH test is the only way to measure this ratio clinically. Low ratio = elevated breast and endometrial cancer risk.

cards.title: Endometriosis — Integrative Management
cards._legacyHtml:
Endometriosis — 10 Years Average Diagnostic Delay
Endometriosis affects 10% of reproductive-age women (176 million globally). Average diagnostic delay is 7–10 years because: (1) period pain is normalised, (2) symptoms overlap with IBS/bladder, (3) laparoscopy required for diagnosis. BNYS role: pain management, anti-inflammatory support, hormone balancing, and quality of life improvement alongside medical/surgical management.
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
Omega-3 3–4g/day★★★ Reduces prostaglandin E2 and leukotriene B4 — primary inflammatory mediators in endometriosis. Reduces dysmenorrhoea comparable to NSAIDs in some RCTs.EPA+DHA 3–4g/day. Start 2 weeks before menstruation for dysmenorrhoea. Continue daily for systemic anti-inflammatory effect.
DIM (diindolylmethane)★★★ Shifts oestrogen metabolism from 16-OH (pro-proliferative) to 2-OH (protective). Reduces ectopic tissue growth in animal models. Human pilot data promising.DIM 200–400mg/day. From cruciferous vegetables (broccoli sprouts, cauliflower) — 500g/day would be required from food; supplement preferred for therapeutic dose.
Resveratrol★★ Inhibits aromatase (reduces local oestrogen production in ectopic tissue), anti-inflammatory, anti-angiogenicResveratrol 30mg/day BD (trans-resveratrol from Japanese knotweed or grape skin extract). Synergistic with curcumin.
Low-FODMAP + anti-inflammatory diet★★★ 72% of endometriosis patients have IBS-type symptoms (shared visceral hypersensitivity). Low-FODMAP reduces bloating and pain. Anti-inflammatory pattern reduces inflammatory load.Mediterranean anti-inflammatory diet base. Low-FODMAP trial 4–6 weeks if GI symptoms prominent. Eliminate: red meat, trans fats, gluten (controversial but reported to help). Add: turmeric, ginger, colourful vegetables.
Pelvic physiotherapy★★★ Addresses hypertonic pelvic floor (common in endo — protective guarding response). Internal manual therapy, trigger points, myofascial release. Often dramatically reduces dyspareunia and pelvic pain.Refer to specialist pelvic floor physiotherapist. 6–12 sessions. Combined with yoga (yin yoga for pelvic release ★★). Home stretching: pigeon, reclined butterfly, supine figure-4.

Post-menopause: <140 pmol/L (stable, low). Perimenopausal: unpredictable.Not reliable for perimenopause diagnosis. Used for monitoring HRT dosing and in PCOS/fertility assessment.
AMH (Anti-Müllerian Hormone)Best single marker of ovarian reserve — reflects remaining primordial follicle poolAge-specific ranges. AMH <0.4 ng/mL = very low ovarian reserve. AMH <1.0 ng/mL = diminished reserve. AMH >3.5 ng/mL = polycystic ovary pattern.Does NOT change throughout the menstrual cycle (unlike FSH/E2) — can be measured any time. Decreases smoothly with age — the best reserve predictor.
DUTCH Test (Dried Urine)Gold standard for full sex hormone profiling — oestrogen, progesterone, testosterone, cortisol, DHEAS, melatonin + metabolitesShows: oestrogen metabolism pathways (2-OH vs 4-OH vs 16-OH oestrogens). Cortisol-DHEA ratio. Complete hormonal picture unavailable from serum.Expensive (₹10,000–15,000 in India). Not universally available. Complex to interpret — requires trained practitioner. Most valuable in: unexplained symptoms with normal serum, assessment for cancer risk oestrogen metabolite profile.
🟡 The Symptom-Based Approach
The most experienced menopause specialists (Menopause Society) advise: in a woman over 45 with typical perimenopause symptoms (irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms, mood changes, sleep disruption), blood tests are not required to make the clinical diagnosis or start treatment. Symptoms + age + menstrual irregularity = perimenopause. Blood tests are indicated for: women under 40 (premature ovarian insufficiency — different management), to guide HRT dosing, to exclude other causes (thyroid, anaemia, pregnancy), and for fertility counselling. Never withhold treatment based on a normal hormone test in a symptomatic perimenopausal woman.

cards.title: Breast Health — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Breast Cancer — Screening is Life-Saving ★
Mammography screening: 40–74 years, every 2 years (India: every year from 40 with high risk). CBE (clinical breast examination) annually. Self-breast examination monthly (7–10 days after period). Any new breast lump, nipple discharge, skin change, or axillary lymphadenopathy → urgent referral.
ConditionFeaturesAssessmentNaturopathic Approach
Fibrocystic breast changesBilateral cyclical breast pain, lumpiness — worse premenstrually. Very common (60% of women)Clinical + USS if discrete lump. Biopsy if suspiciousEvening primrose oil 3g/day (GLA — Grade B), Vit E 400IU, reduce caffeine, low-fat diet, Vit B6 100mg
Mastalgia (breast pain)Cyclical (hormonal) or non-cyclical (musculoskeletal, ductal)Pain diary — cyclical vs non-cyclical, mammogram if >35Evening primrose oil, Vit B6, well-fitting supportive bra, Vit E, reduce dietary fat (saturated), danazol (prescriptive for severe)
Galactorrhoea (non-puerperal)Milky nipple discharge when not pregnant/breastfeedingProlactin level, MRI pituitary (r/o prolactinoma), TSH (hypothyroidism)Identify cause — if medication-induced (antipsychotics, metoclopramide) review with prescriber. Vitex (Chaste Tree) if hormonal — reduces prolactin
Breast cancer preventionLifestyle risk reductionBRCA1/2 testing if family history. BIRADS scoring on imagingMediterranean diet (↓ risk 25%), exercise 150 min/week (↓ risk 20%), maintain healthy weight (post-menopausal obesity ↑ oestrogen), limit alcohol (each drink/day ↑ risk 7%), breastfeed if possible
🟡 Phytoestrogens & Breast Cancer — Current Evidence
Phytoestrogens (soy isoflavones) were historically feared in ER+ breast cancer — concern that they would stimulate tumour growth. Current evidence is reassuring: dietary soy (tofu, edamame, miso) is safe and may be PROTECTIVE — Asian women who eat soy throughout life have lower breast cancer rates. However, high-dose soy supplements in ER+ breast cancer patients should be discussed with the oncologist before use. The evidence for harm is from animal studies with isolated isoflavones at very high doses — not from dietary soy. Key message: whole food soy = safe. High-dose isolated isoflavone supplements in ER+ cancer = discuss with oncologist first.

cards.title: Breast Health & Female Osteoporosis
cards._legacyHtml:
BREAST HEALTH
Breast Cancer Screening ★
Mammography every 2 years from age 40. CBE annually. Self-breast examination monthly (days 7–10 after period). Any new lump, nipple discharge, skin change → urgent referral.
ConditionFeaturesNaturopathic Approach
Fibrocystic breast changesBilateral cyclical pain, lumpiness — worse premenstrually. Very common (60% of women)Evening primrose oil 3g/day (GLA), Vit E 400IU, reduce caffeine, Vit B6 100mg, low-fat diet
Mastalgia (cyclical)Premenstrual breast pain — hormonalEvening primrose oil, Vitex 400mg AM, well-fitting supportive bra, reduce dietary fat
Breast cancer preventionLifestyle risk reductionMediterranean diet (↓ risk 25%), exercise 150 min/week (↓ risk 20%), maintain healthy BMI, alcohol <1 drink/day (each drink ↑ risk 7%), breastfeed if possible
🟡 Phytoestrogens & ER+ Breast Cancer — Current Evidence
Dietary soy (tofu, edamame, miso) is SAFE and likely PROTECTIVE in ER+ breast cancer — Asian women with lifelong soy consumption have lower breast cancer rates. High-dose isolated isoflavone supplements in active ER+ cancer treatment: discuss with oncologist before prescribing. Phytoestrogens bind ER with lower affinity than oestradiol and act as partial agonists/antagonists — dietary amounts are not a concern.
OSTEOPOROSIS IN WOMEN
InterventionDoseEvidence
Calcium ★1200mg/day total (food + supplement) in split doses★★★★ — food sources preferred (dairy, sardines, tofu, leafy greens)
Vitamin D3 ★2000–4000 IU/day — target 25(OH)D >75 nmol/L★★★★ — must combine with calcium
Vitamin K2 (MK-7)100–200mcg/day★★★ — directs calcium to bone, away from arteries
Resistance training ★2–3x/week all major muscle groups★★★★ — mechanical loading activates osteoblasts
Collagen peptides10g/day★★★ — improves BMD + muscle mass in postmenopausal women (RCT)
Isoflavones (post-menopausal)80–90mg/day soy isoflavones★★★ — oestrogenic effect reduces bone resorption

cards.title: Osteoporosis in Women — Risk, DEXA & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Osteoporosis — Silent Disease Until Fracture
1 in 3 women over 50 will have an osteoporotic fracture. Hip fracture 1-year mortality is 20-30%. Prevention starts decades before — peak bone mass is achieved at age 25-30, then gradually declines.
Risk FactorModifiableNon-Modifiable
Major risksLow calcium and Vit D intake, smoking, alcohol, low body weight, inactivity, long-term steroids or PPIsAge, female sex, menopause, prior fragility fracture, family history, white or Asian ethnicity
Secondary causesCoeliac disease, IBD, hyperparathyroidism, thyrotoxicosis, rheumatoid arthritis, T1DM, early menopause (under 45), prolonged amenorrhoea, aromatase inhibitors
InterventionDoseEvidence
Calcium1200mg/day total (food preferred + supplement). Max 500mg per dose.Strong — with Vit D3
Vitamin D32000-4000 IU/day. Target 25(OH)D above 75 nmol/L.Strong — essential with calcium
Vitamin K2 (MK-7)100-200mcg/dayGood — directs calcium to bone, prevents vascular calcification
Magnesium300-400mg/dayGood — cofactor for Vit D activation and bone matrix proteins
Resistance training2-3x/week all major muscle groupsStrong — most potent stimulus for bone formation (mechanical loading activates osteoblasts)
Collagen peptides10g/dayGood — improves bone mineral density and muscle mass in postmenopausal women (RCT)
Soy isoflavones80-90mg/day (postmenopausal)Good — oestrogenic effect on bone, reduces resorption
🟡 FRAX Score
Use FRAX calculator (shef.ac.uk/FRAX) for every patient with osteopenia or risk factors. FRAX calculates 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture and hip fracture — guides treatment decisions. Consider bisphosphonates if 10-year hip fracture risk over 3% or major fracture risk over 20%, alongside naturopathic protocol. Always investigate and treat secondary causes first — untreated coeliac disease or hyperparathyroidism makes osteoporosis treatment ineffective.

cards.title: Cervical Health & HPV — Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Cervical Cancer — 99% Caused by HPV — Preventable ★
HPV (Human Papillomavirus) — especially strains 16 and 18 — causes 99% of cervical cancers. Most HPV infections clear spontaneously within 2 years. Persistent infection → cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) → cancer over 10–20 years.
ScreeningScheduleNotes
Pap smear (cytology)Every 3 years from age 21 (or 3 years after first sexual activity)India: every 3 years in public health. Private: annually recommended
HPV co-testEvery 5 years from age 30 (Pap + HPV DNA)Preferred by current guidelines — higher sensitivity than Pap alone
HPV vaccination9–26 years (2 doses if <15, 3 doses if >15). Some guidelines extend to 45Gardasil 9 — protects against 9 HPV strains including 16, 18, 6, 11. Safe and highly effective — 98% protection
Naturopathic SupportDoseEvidenceMechanism
Folate (methylfolate)800mcg–5mg/day★★★ GoodDNA methylation and repair — folate deficiency impairs HPV clearance
Vitamin C1–2g/day★★★ GoodAntiviral, immune support, cervical mucosal integrity
Vitamin A (beta-carotene)10,000 IU beta-carotene/day (NOT retinol — teratogenic)★★★ GoodEpithelial differentiation — deficiency associated with CIN progression
Green tea (EGCG)3–5 cups/day or EGCG 400mg/day. Topical EGCG ointment for CIN 2 (prescriptive)★★★ GoodEGCG inhibits HPV E6/E7 oncoproteins — promotes apoptosis of abnormal cells
Indole-3-carbinol (DIM)200–300mg/day★★★ GoodPromotes favourable oestrogen metabolism (2-OHE1 over 16-OHE1), reduces CIN regression time
Immune supportZinc 15mg + Selenium 200mcg + Vit D 4000IU★★★ GoodImmune clearance of HPV — low immunity = persistent infection
🟡 CIN Management — When to Refer
CIN 1 = watchful waiting + naturopathic support (60% spontaneous regression at 2 years). CIN 2 = naturopathic support + 6-monthly colposcopy. Regression possible especially in women under 30. CIN 3 = REFER immediately for LLETZ/cone biopsy — risk of progression to cancer is too high to delay. Key lifestyle factors that promote HPV clearance: smoking cessation (smoking impairs local cervical immunity — most important modifiable factor), safe sex practices, immune support, stress management. Naturopathy can genuinely help CIN 1–2 regression — this is an area of real clinical opportunity.

cards.title: Pelvic Floor Health — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Pelvic Floor — The Hidden Foundation
The pelvic floor is a hammock of muscles supporting bladder, uterus, and rectum. Dysfunction affects 1 in 3 women who have given birth and is massively underdiagnosed. It causes: UI, pelvic organ prolapse, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and fecal incontinence — all treatable.
ConditionFeaturesPelvic Floor StatusApproach
Stress urinary incontinenceLeakage with exertion/cough/sneezeUnderactive/weakKegel exercises, pelvic floor PT, pessary, weight loss
Urge incontinence (OAB)Sudden urge, cannot delay voidingOveractive detrusorBladder training, pelvic floor PT, neuromodulation
Pelvic organ prolapsePressure/heaviness in pelvis, "something coming down", worse at end of dayWeak + laxPelvic floor PT, pessary (ring pessary), surgical repair if severe (Grades 3–4)
VaginismusInvoluntary vaginal muscle spasm — penetration impossible or painful, primary or secondaryHypertonic (too tight)Pelvic floor PT + psychosexual therapy + gradual dilator therapy. Never Kegels — worsens hypertonic floor
Vulvodynia/dyspareuniaChronic vulval pain, burning, stinging — no identifiable causeOften hypertonic + neural sensitisationPelvic floor PT, low-oxalate diet, topical lidocaine, amitriptyline (prescriptive), CBT for pain
K
Kegel Exercise Protocol (for Underactive Floor) Identify: squeeze as if stopping urinary stream AND holding wind simultaneously. 3-second hold × 10 reps (fast twitch) + 10-second hold × 10 reps (slow twitch) = 1 set. 3 sets/day. Progress weekly. IMPORTANT: only for underactive/weak floors — contraindicated in hypertonic pelvic floor (vaginismus, vulvodynia, chronic pelvic pain).
N
Naturopathic Support for Pelvic Health Magnesium glycinate 300mg — reduces detrusor overactivity and pelvic muscle tension. Vit D 4000 IU — pelvic floor muscle function. Omega-3 3g — reduces pelvic neuroinflammation. Pumpkin seed extract 500mg BD — Grade B for OAB. Low-oxalate diet for vulvodynia (oxalate crystals irritate vulval tissue). Topical coconut oil — safe lubricant, reduces dryness and friction.
🟡 Refer to Pelvic Floor Physiotherapist
Pelvic floor physiotherapy is the most evidence-based treatment for most pelvic floor disorders — superior to general physiotherapy and equally effective as surgery for Grade 1–2 prolapse. Unfortunately massively under-utilised. Criteria for referral: any leakage affecting quality of life, prolapse symptoms, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, vaginismus, post-partum (should be routine at 6-week check), post-prostatectomy (men). The assessment involves internal examination — ensure patient is prepared and consents fully. A trained pelvic floor physiotherapist can distinguish hypertonic from hypotonic floor — critical for correct treatment.

cards.title: Vaginal Infections, Contraception & Sexual Health
cards._legacyHtml:
VAGINAL INFECTIONS
ConditionDischargepHDiagnosisTreatment
Bacterial vaginosis (BV)Thin, grey-white, fishy odour (worse after intercourse)>4.5Amsel criteria (3 of 4): discharge, pH >4.5, clue cells, whiff test. Or Nugent score on Gram stainMetronidazole 400mg BD × 7 days (prescriptive). Naturopathic: boric acid 600mg vaginal caps × 14 nights, probiotics L. reuteri RC-14 + L. rhamnosus GR-1 oral BD × 4 weeks
Vulvovaginal candidiasisThick, white, cottage cheese — no odour<4.5 (normal)Clinical + HVS (C&S). Microscopy: hyphae/pseudohyphaeClotrimazole topical or fluconazole oral 150mg single dose (prescriptive). Naturopathic: boric acid 600mg vaginal caps × 14 days (evidence-based especially for non-albicans Candida), probiotics, low-sugar diet
Trichomoniasis (STI)Green-yellow, frothy, offensive>4.5HVS microscopy (motile trichomonads) or NAATMetronidazole 2g single dose or 400mg BD × 7 days. TREAT PARTNER. Notify STI.
Atrophic vaginitis (menopause)Scanty, watery, burning, dryness>5 (oestrogen deficiency)Clinical — post-menopausal, dyspareunia, recurrent UTITopical oestrogen (prescriptive). Naturopathic: sea buckthorn oil 3g orally ★, topical Vit E oil, coconut oil, pelvic floor PT, probiotics
CONTRACEPTION — NATUROPATHIC PERSPECTIVE
MethodNaturopathic Consideration
Combined OCPDepletes: B6, B12, folate, Mg, zinc, Vit C, CoQ10. Supplement throughout. Increases: triglycerides, copper. Stopping OCP: may trigger telogen effluvium (hair loss 3–4 months later) — support with iron, zinc, biotin.
Progesterone-onlyFewer nutrient depletions. May cause irregular bleeding — Vitex agnus-castus may help regulate.
IUD (copper)May increase menstrual bleeding and cramping — omega-3 3g, Mg, Vit B1 reduce dysmenorrhoea.

cards.title: Vaginal Infections — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionDischargepHKey TestTreatment
Bacterial vaginosis (BV)Grey-white, thin, fishy odour (worse after sex)>4.5Amsel criteria (3 of 4: discharge, pH >4.5, clue cells on microscopy, positive whiff test) or NAATMetronidazole 400mg BD x7 days or 2g single dose (prescriptive). Probiotic L.rhamnosus + L.reuteri vaginal or oral — reduces recurrence. Boric acid 600mg vaginal capsules 14 days for recurrent BV.
Candidal vulvovaginitisWhite, thick, cottage cheese — no odour<4.5 (normal)High vaginal swab + culture. pH normal distinguishes from BV.Clotrimazole 500mg pessary single dose or oral fluconazole 150mg single dose (prescriptive). Naturopathic: boric acid 600mg vaginal suppository OD x14 days (evidence for recurrent — especially non-albicans Candida). Probiotics. Reduce dietary sugar and refined carbs. Tea tree oil sitz bath (2 drops in warm water — not undiluted).
TrichomoniasisYellow-green, frothy, malodorous>4.5Wet mount (motile trichomonads), NAAT (most sensitive)Metronidazole 2g single dose (BOTH partners must be treated). STI — refer for full STI screen. Avoid alcohol during treatment.
Vaginal atrophy (GSM)Minimal, watery, dryness, dyspareunia, urinary symptoms>5Clinical — post-menopausal contextTopical oestrogen (gold standard, minimal systemic absorption). Naturopathic: sea buckthorn oil 3g/day oral (RCT — improves vaginal epithelium), topical Vit E, coconut oil lubricant, hyaluronic acid vaginal gel.
🟡 Recurrent Candida — Investigation Protocol
Recurrent candida (4+ episodes/year) requires investigation: blood glucose and HbA1c (undiagnosed T2DM is most common cause), HIV testing, iron deficiency (impairs mucosal immunity), thyroid function, check all medications (antibiotics, steroids, immunosuppressants, OCP). Culture the organism — 15% of recurrent cases are non-albicans species (C.glabrata, C.krusei) which are inherently resistant to fluconazole but sensitive to boric acid 600mg vaginal capsules. Probiotic L.rhamnosus GR-1 + L.reuteri RC-14 — specific strains with evidence for vaginal microbiome restoration.

cards.title: Contraception — Naturopathic Perspective & Effects
cards._legacyHtml:
Naturopathic Role in Contraception
BNYS practitioners do not prescribe contraception but must understand its effects, nutritional implications, and how to support patients on hormonal contraception. Many patients also seek naturopathic alternatives or wish to transition off hormonal contraception.
MethodNutritional DepletionsNaturopathic SupportSide Effects to Address
Combined oral contraceptive pill (OCP)B6, B12, folate, zinc, magnesium, selenium, CoQ10, Vit CB-complex daily, zinc 15mg, magnesium 300mg, Vit C 500mg, CoQ10 100mg throughout OCP useMood changes (B6, Mg), libido (zinc, DHEA), headaches (magnesium), nausea (ginger, B6)
Progestogen-only pill (POP/Mini-pill)Similar to OCP but less severeB-complex, zinc, magnesiumIrregular bleeding, mood, acne — DIM/I3C for oestrogen metabolism
Hormonal IUD (Mirena)Minimal — localised progestogenMonitor iron (can cause or worsen anaemia, or improve heavy periods)Irregular spotting (iron monitoring), mood effects in sensitive women
Copper IUDMay increase menstrual blood loss — ironMonitor ferritin annually. Iron bisglycinate if ferritin low. Zinc if deficient.Heavier periods — see menorrhagia naturopathic protocol
Natural family planning (NFP)NoneCycle tracking (Creighton, Billings, symptothermal method), basal body temperature, LH testing. Effectiveness 76-98% depending on method and correct use.N/A — requires consistent tracking and abstinence during fertile window
🟡 Post-Pill Syndrome
After stopping OCP, some women experience: amenorrhoea (up to 6 months), acne rebound, PMS intensification, hair shedding (telogen effluvium from hormonal shift), mood changes, and nutritional deficiency symptoms. Support protocol: B-complex, zinc, magnesium, Vitex (after 3 months off pill — regulates cycle), seed cycling (flaxseed and pumpkin follicular phase; sesame and sunflower luteal phase — modest hormonal modulation). Most symptoms resolve within 3-6 months. Prolactin level if amenorrhoea persists beyond 6 months.

cards.title: Sexual Health & Female Libido — Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Female Sexual Dysfunction — Underdiagnosed
Female sexual interest/arousal disorder (FSIAD) affects 40% of women at some point. Low libido, dyspareunia (painful sex), anorgasmia. Always explore: hormonal, psychological, relational, medication, and anatomical factors.
CauseAssessmentNaturopathic Intervention
Hormonal (low testosterone)Total and free testosterone, SHBG, DHEA-S. Free testosterone often low even with normal total (high SHBG binds testosterone).DHEA 25-50mg/day (SHBG ↓, free testosterone ↑). Vitex (lowers prolactin if elevated). Tribulus terrestris 250mg TDS (modest libido evidence). Zinc 15mg (testosterone cofactor).
Menopausal (atrophy and dryness)GSM symptoms — dryness, dyspareunia, vulval atrophySea buckthorn oil 3g/day oral (RCT — improves vaginal mucosa). Topical Vit E. Coconut oil or hyaluronic acid lubricant. Topical oestrogen (refer GP — gold standard).
OCP-inducedSHBG elevated on OCP (binds testosterone) — libido often returns after stoppingZinc, B6, Mg during OCP. Consider non-hormonal contraception if libido significantly impaired.
Antidepressant-inducedSSRIs cause sexual dysfunction in 50-70% — anorgasmia, low desireMaca root 3g/day (RCT — specifically reverses SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction). Discuss with prescriber about switching to bupropion or mirtazapine (lower sexual side effect profile).
Psychological/relationalRelationship quality, trauma history, body image, anxiety, depressionCBT for sexual dysfunction, EMDR for sexual trauma, couples therapy, mindfulness-based sex therapy (Brotto protocol). Refer psychosexual therapist.
Dyspareunia (painful sex)Pelvic examination (vaginismus, atrophy, endometriosis, infections)Pelvic floor physiotherapy, lubricants, treat underlying cause. Never ignore dyspareunia — always assess.

cards.title: HRT — Risks, Benefits & Naturopathic Context
cards._legacyHtml:
HRT — Re-Evaluation After the WHI Study Clarification
The 2002 WHI study caused massive HRT abandonment — but the study used oral conjugated equine oestrogen + medroxyprogesterone acetate in older (average age 63) post-menopausal women, not perimenopausal women starting HRT at menopause. Re-analysis showed dramatically different risk-benefit profile for women starting HRT within 10 years of menopause onset ("timing hypothesis"). The conversation has fundamentally changed.
FactorDetailClinical Implication
Timing HypothesisHRT started within 10 years of menopause or before age 60 = cardiovascular BENEFIT (not risk). Started >10 years after = possible cardiovascular risk.Perimenopausal women asking about HRT: timing strongly favours benefit for bone, heart, cognitive function, and vasomotor symptoms. Refer to experienced menopause clinician.
Route of administrationTransdermal oestrogen: no ↑ VTE risk (oral oestrogen = 2–3× ↑ VTE, transdermal = neutral for VTE risk)Transdermal (patch, gel) preferred over oral for VTE safety. Especially important in women with obesity, smoking, thrombophilia risk.
Progestogen typeMicronised progesterone (Utrogestan/Prometrium): neutral cardiovascular profile, no ↑ breast cancer vs synthetic progestogens (MPA). WHI used MPA — the problematic progestogen.Body-identical progesterone preferred over synthetic progestogens. Micronised progesterone also improves sleep and anxiety.
Breast cancer riskOestrogen-only HRT (hysterectomised): slightly DECREASES breast cancer risk. Combined HRT: small increase (equivalent to: drinking 1–2 glasses wine/night, BMI >30, having no children, or being sedentary)Contextualise breast cancer risk: other modifiable lifestyle factors (alcohol, obesity, inactivity) carry comparable or higher risk than combined HRT started at appropriate age.
1
Naturopathic Alternatives & Adjuncts to HRT Phytoestrogens (non-HRT alternatives): Genistein (soy) 54mg/day ★★★ (reduces hot flushes 50% vs 25% placebo — only isoflavone with consistent RCT evidence). Equol (soy metabolite — only produced by ~50% of women with right gut bacteria) ★★★. Red clover isoflavones 40–80mg ★★. Flaxseed 25g/day ★★ (phytoestrogenic lignans + omega-3 + fibre). Remifemin (black cohosh extract) 40mg BD ★★★ (KappaE-ligand mechanism — not oestrogenic — safe in ER+ cancer per current evidence). Ashwagandha ★★ (reduces hot flush severity via HPA modulation, improves sleep). Lifestyle: weight loss (reduces oestrogen from adipose conversion of androgens → reduces hot flushes). Exercise ★★★ (most consistent lifestyle intervention for vasomotor symptom reduction). Yoga ★★★.
